summary: The main claim is: I think this model addresses the core concerns of the anti-AI regulation folks: the concentration of power and the freedom of general political and ethical views.

top_comment.author: Artem Karpov
top_comment.text: What do you think are realistic ways to enforce this on a global level? It seems UN can't enforce regulations world widely, USA and EU work in their areas only. Others can catch up but somewhat unlikely to do it.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: What do you think are realistic ways to enforce this on a global level?

summary: The main claim is: Disagreeing with Eliezer Y., Nora Belrose calls the Orthogonality Thesis "trivial, false or meaningless/confused".
Clearly the orthogonality thesis is nontrivially useful for cases like this.
The main claim is: AI agents therefore pose a unique risk: people could build AIs that pursue dangerous goals.
Unilateral actors considerably increase the risks of malicious use.

top_comment.author: 1a3orn
top_comment.text:
None of the following suggestions seem to concern themselves with limiting government's surveillance powers, so this sentence about "finding a balance" seems rather misleading. You have 4 classes of "suggestions," which all concern individual misuse or corporate misuse. Perhaps you left some out by accident?

Furthermore, some of your suggestions would almost certainly increase corporate influence / government power. For instance:

[...]

If pursued fully, this measure would cripple or render impossible open-source AI of any capability at all, rather as requiring a car company / gun company / knife company to show that its products cannot be misused would (obviously) render impossible a car company's / gun company's / knife company's services. Making open source AI impossible in turn increases corporate power because they'll be the only ones permitted to sell AI, which in turn increases centralization. And because corporations are easier to keep a hand on and surveil, this in turn increases government power.

Maybe you think that's worth it for other reasons! But that's a trade-off that should be addressed explicitly.

Government's using AI to maximize their power is a bad consequence that can happen even without great breakthroughs in AI, or speculative AI recursive-self improvement. It's worth thinking about and making plans to avoid. I'm really happy that you highlight it as a potential problem, but I think the measures you propose don't actually help avoid it in any way -- they all tilt in the direction of greater centralization.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: I'm really happy that you highlight it as a potential problem, but I think the measures you propose don't actually help avoid it in any way -- they all tilt in the direction of greater centralization.

summary: The main claim is: I think people in this group tend to have somewhat more optimistic views on how likely things are to go well, but this isn't a super strong correlation.
I tend to bucket those advocating for regulations into three buckets.
The main claim is: This structure could potentially benefit ASIs as well as humans.
Moreover, even if ASIs retain humanity due to the value of markets, they would likely control most available resources.
The main claim is: Of the structure in the dark matter shadow of hand-holdable structure, some is itself hand-holdable.

The main claim is: If AGI is built as brain-like model-based RL, we may be able to steer its motivations by exploiting how it forms values — analogous to how a high-status person can turn someone into a 'moon fanboy' — giving a workable if 'mediocre' alignment plan.
This is only the least-bad plan Byrnes currently has; it aims for a future much better than paperclips, not an optimally-good one, and he can't show it would actually work.

top_comment.author: Bogdan Ionut Cirstea
top_comment.text: Valence (and arousal) also seem relatively easy to learn even for current models e.g. The Perceptual Primacy of Feeling: Affectless machine vision models robustly predict human visual arousal, valence, and aesthetics; Quantifying Valence and Arousal in Text with Multilingual Pre-trained Transformers. And abstract concepts like 'human flourishing' could be relatively easy to learn even just from text e.g. Language is more abstract than you think, or, why aren't languages more iconic?; Artificial neural network language models align neurally and behaviorally with humans even after a developmentally realistic amount of training.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: The Perceptual Primacy of Feeling: Affectless machine vision models robustly predict human visual arousal, valence, and aesthetics; Quantifying Valence and Arousal in Text with Multilingual Pre-trained Transformers.

summary: The main claim is: Deceptive Alignment ---------------------- Consider also deceptive alignment.
The main claim is: More local meanings are more robust than more distal meanings.
The main claim is: Therefore, the scope and precision of its knowledge about the real world may be a factor of its dangerousness.
Computing power: The more computing power a system has, the more powerful, and therefore potentially dangerous, it becomes.
